variables.name: net_income
variables.description: Income after cost, deposits, rent, platform fees, and time cost.

variables.name: compliance_risk
variables.description: License, platform rule, contract, and work constraint risk.

variables.name: health_load
variables.description: Sleep, physical load, accident risk, and long-term sustainability.

variables.name: maintenance_cost
variables.description: Learning, equipment, deposits, accounts, and time management.

variables.name: reversibility
variables.description: Ability to exit after trial without high sunk cost.

assumptions: Prefer low maintenance, low cost, and low legal risk.
Do not assume high deposit, vehicle purchase, or long-term heavy labor is acceptable unless explicitly allowed.
Version one uses structural modeling first and does not disguise language reasoning as strict numerical simulation.
Hard avoid constraint: large vehicle purchase.
Hard avoid constraint: long daytime work after night shift.
Hard avoid constraint: high accident exposure.
Hard avoid constraint: unclear platform penalties.
causal_chain: Environment and constraints define the option space.
Resources, cost, compliance, and physical load shape net return and sustainability.
High-uncertainty variables require warning indicators and stop-loss rules.
The final path should prefer low legal risk, low sunk cost, and reversible testing.

scenario_tree.base_case: Use conservative assumptions and validate with a small trial.
scenario_tree.upside_case: If key variables improve, scale only after evidence is collected.
scenario_tree.downside_case: If return, compliance, or health variables worsen, trigger stop-loss.

risk_factors.control: Require red-team objections and disconfirming variables.
Include deposits, equipment, commute, and sleep loss in total cost.
Expose uncertainty instead of pretending certainty.

decision_options.description: Keep the current path as a low-risk baseline.
Run a low-deposit, short-cycle, reversible test.
Reject high-deposit, illegal, or sleep-damaging routes.

mesa_recommendation.reason: Delivery and ride-hailing earnings depend on many workers competing for limited orders and time windows.

uncertainties: Without real cost and demand data, the result is structured judgment rather than strict numerical simulation.
External model output is evidence or review input, not final truth.

mesa_result.reason: Delivery and ride-hailing earnings depend on many workers competing for limited orders and time windows.

summary.interpretation: With limited demand, returns can diverge under competition and random allocation.

warnings: This is a first-version minimal Mesa-compatible runner, not an industry-grade simulation.

  "summary": "For a Fuzhou night-shift security guard seeking sustainable side income with low upfront cost, low fatigue, low accident exposure, and low exit cost, grocery delivery (Pupu) is the best option. It requires a personal e-bike (low cost), offers predictable hours with minimal sleep disruption, has lower accident risk than ride-hailing or food delivery, and no long-term contracts. Food delivery is a secondary option, while ride-hailing is unsuitable due to high vehicle costs and accident exposure. The baseline security job provides stable income but limited growth. Local verification of platform policies and order density is essential.",

red_team.summary: The planner and modeler assume a fixed 4-hour side job window (3-7pm) with moderate order demand for delivery and low for ride-hailing. Weak assumption: actual Fuzhou afternoon demand for ride-hailing may spike near rush hour (5-7pm). Disconfirming evidence: PuPu grocery delivery often requires heavier loads and longer parking lot stops, increasing fatigue and time per order, potentially reducing net hourly income to 10-15 RMB. The modeler's 20-30 RMB estimate for PuPu is optimistic. Also, the assumption that the guard owns an e-bike with 40km range may fail; many guards rely on public transport or walking, making grocery delivery infeasible without upfront purchase. The 'zero exit cost' assumption ignores potential platform penalties for missed orders or low ratings, which could impose hidden costs.

risks.risk: Order demand mismatch with assumed window
risks.mitigation: Verify real-time order density via platform trial before committing.

risks.risk: PuPu grocery delivery load/fatigue higher than modeled
risks.mitigation: Test only lightweight orders; weigh physical exertion vs. earnings.

risks.risk: E-bike ownership and range assumption false
risks.mitigation: Confirm user actually owns a charged e-bike; if not, exclude PuPu.
Read platform contract carefully; choose no-deposit platforms.

red_team.recommendation: Conduct a one-week trial starting with food delivery (Meituan) instead of PuPu, as it offers clearer earnings per order, lower physical load, and easier exit. Re-evaluate after 3 days of trial; switch if net hourly income <20 RMB or fatigue high.

judge.summary:
{
  "summary": "After weighing planner, modeler, and red team analyses, the best sustainable side income for a Fuzhou night-shift security guard is food delivery (Meituan) over grocery delivery (Pupu) or ride-hailing. Food delivery balances moderate entry cost (insulated bag ~200 RMB), manageable fatigue in a 4-hour afternoon window, lower accident exposure than ride-hailing, and easy exit. The red team correctly identified that grocery delivery may have higher physical load and lower net hourly income (10-15 RMB vs. optimistic 20-30), and e-bike ownership cannot be assumed. Ride-hailing is ruled out due to high vehicle cost and accident risk. The baseline job is stable but offers no growth. A one-week trial is essential to verify actual earnings, order density, and fatigue.",
  "risks": [
    {"risk": "Order demand may be low in Fuzhou afternoon (3-7pm), especially for ride-hailing", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Verify real-time order density via platform trial before committing."},
    {"risk": "Grocery delivery (Pupu) loads and parking lot stops increase fatigue and reduce net hourly income to 10-15 RMB", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Start with food delivery instead; re-evaluate if physical strain is too high."},
    {"risk": "User may not own a charged e-bike, making delivery options infeasible without upfront purchase", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Confirm e-bike availability; if not, explore walking deliveries or reconsider baseline."},
    {"risk": "Hidden platform penalties (cancellation, late delivery, low ratings) could reduce net income", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Choose platforms with transparent rules and no upfront deposits."},
    {"risk": "Fatigue from overlapping night-shift and side job could impact health and job performance", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Limit side job to 4 hours/day (3-7pm) after 7 hours sleep; stop if fatigue affects security performance."}
  ],
  "recommendation": "Conduct a one-week trial starting with food delivery (Meituan) using an e-bike (if owned) or a rented e-bike. Work 4 hours each afternoon after sleep (3-7pm). Monitor net hourly income (target ≥20 RMB) and fatigue level (scale 1-5). If after 3 days net hourly <15 RMB or fatigue >3 (high), switch to grocery delivery (Pupu) or revert to baseline. Avoid ride-hailing entirely. If e-bike is unavailable, food delivery becomes high-cost; then baseline or walking-based gigs may be best. Recheck after one week and decide whether to continue, modify, or stop.",
  "
